title: Pakistani Chicken Jalfrezi

ingredients.name: chicken breast or boneless thigh, cut in 3 cm cubes

subs.name: 600 g paneer (for vegetarian version)
subs.notes: cut into same-sized cubes; cook time same

ingredients.name: capsicum (green, red, or mix), cut into strips

subs.name: 200 g broccoli florets
subs.notes: add slightly earlier as broccoli takes longer to soften

steps.text: Season the chicken cubes with salt, half the red chilli powder, and half the turmeric. Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wide karahi or wok over very high heat. Add the chicken and stir-fry for 5–6 minutes, turning often, until every side is seared golden. Remove the chicken and set aside.

Add the remaining oil to the same pan. Add cumin seeds, then add ginger-garlic paste and fry for 1 minute. Add the chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, remaining chilli powder, coriander powder, remaining turmeric, and salt. Stir vigorously on high heat for 4 minutes until the tomatoes break down into a thick paste.

Add the onion petals and slit green chillies to the masala. Stir-fry on high heat for 2 minutes — the onions should soften slightly at the edges but retain their crunch. The colour will brighten and the raw smell of the masala will fade.

Return the seared chicken to the pan. Stir well to coat every piece in the masala. Add the capsicum strips and toss everything together over high heat for 3–4 minutes until the capsicum is just tender but still bright and slightly crisp.
Squeeze lemon juice over the dish, sprinkle garam masala, and toss once more. The sauce should be thick and clinging, not pooling — if it looks too dry, add 2 tablespoons of water and toss quickly.
Garnish with fresh coriander and serve immediately with naan, chapati, or egg fried rice. Jalfrezi must be eaten hot — it does not keep well as the vegetables lose their texture.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 600 g paneer (for vegetarian version) (cut into same-sized cubes; cook time same); capsicum → 200 g broccoli florets (add slightly earlier as broccoli takes longer to soften); tomato-paste → extra 1 tomato, grated (reduces thickness slightly)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Jalfrezi is made identically in Indian kitchens and needs no modification. Use a flat-bottomed wok or cast-iron kadai to achieve the high-heat stir-fry. The dish works brilliantly with Indian paneer as a vegetarian swap — a recipe beloved in north India. Tomato paste (Kissan or Maggi brand) is available in all Indian supermarkets and gives the tangy depth the dish needs.
